item.dc:creator: TheWoodsAreVile

item.title: The most successful animal you may never heard of
item.description:
No other animal has made up so much of the fauna on Earth as the genus Lystrosaurus once did, several million years before the first dinosaurs appeared
Despite appearances were Lystrosaurus not reptiles but therapsids. As therapsids they belonged to the same linage as, though were not themselves ancestors of, mammals (art by John Sibbick)
Various therapsid kindreds dominated the later part of the Permian period, 298.9 - 252.2 million years ago [MYA]. At the time were most landmasses joined into Pangea
Lystrosaurus species varied in size but are often compared to pigs. They had only two teeth but are presumed to had a horny beak to feed on plants with
They had a semi-sprawling gait and could wander far; and their strong forelimbs suggests they could dig for food as well as to nest in burrows
The Permian period ended with volcanism in Siberia for ca 2 million years, that caused Earth's most severe annihilation: the Permian–Triassic extinction event ended 83% of genera, 81% marine species and 70% of vertebrate species on land, as well as many insects and plants
Lystrosaurus lived across Pangea and fossils have been found in what is today India, Mongolia, China, European Russia, Antarctica and South Africa
The fossils were used as evidence by scientists in the 1960s for plate tectonics and that the continents once were connected
The now mostly empty world belonged to Lystrosaurus which prospered. Why these herbivores endured the Great Dying is debated; some suggest it was the abilities to dig and borrow, others to wander far, though some have called it simply luck
Yet no luck lasts forever: new competitors and predators evolved and about 248 MYA, between two and four million years after the Great Dying, the end came to Lystrosaurus. They left no descendants but a far less prosperous relative, Kannemeyeriiformes, diversified during the Triassic period. Its late descendants included Placerias (230 - 215 MYA) and Lisowicia (210 - 205 MYA), whence the reign of the dinosaurs began
item.dc:creator: Ngugi

item.title: Exactly
item.description: It's truly a privilege to not be affected by a major disease while not realizing the reason why you're not being affected by that disease while also taking the who-cares-anymore approach to the very existence of the disease lol

item.title: The way things are currently is unacceptable.
item.description: In case you didn't see it on the front page or User Sub the other day, here is a link to a post I shared the other day with a super useful tip for dealing with insurance companies when they try to deny a claim by saying the treatment/medicine/etc "isn't medically necessary". There were a few commenters who expressed a disbelief that it actually works, but I was unable to update the description with clarification that I know from first hand experience that it DOES work. I've had to do it FOUR times this year, so I know from experience that it does in fact work as long as you have your information organized and ready to go right off the bat. https://imgur.com/gallery/share-this-if-you-live-us-could-save-you-loved-one-ton-of-pain-suffering-worse-Slq0LOL

Like what we do and how we do it? Leave a tip. Or don't. It's totally cool either way. Stay True & Stay Tuned - Meta Pathos

item.title: NO! THIS IS...Patrick?
item.description: TIL: Basket Stars belongs to the order of brittle stars, which are relatives of sea stars. They have five arms that branch into many smaller arms, forming a net-like shape. They use their arms to catch small animals that float in the water, such as crustaceans and jellyfish. They can live up to 35 years and weigh up to 5 kilograms. They are usually found in deep sea habitats or cold waters, but some species can be seen in shallow tropical reefs at night

item.dc:creator: LincLoud

item.title: I've been lying to everyone since Saturday...
item.description:
This is going to be long, because I just need to get it all out. About a year ago, after being a member of the local art gallery for a few months, I took a chance and applied for my own exhibit, and was awarded one for December/January of 2024/2025. I couldn't believe it, and was quite excited at first. My medium is scale miniatures. I fully admit that when compared to other mediums, it's not nearly as impressive. I also know that when it comes to this medium, there are much more proficient and accomplished artists (I've posted some of my work on Imgur). My goal, however, was to attempt to show that scale models are a legitimate medium in an artistic sense. So for the past year I've been building things and deciding what I would put in the gallery. They have a limited number of pedestals to display three dimensional art, like pottery and sculpture, so I limited myself to 10 pieces, 2 of which didn't require pedestals. I felt this was sufficient and a good representation of my work. The event space would be shared with another artist. He paints with acrylic and oils mainly, and his work is AMAZING. Most of it is abstract but involves anything from pop culture references to landscapes, psychedelic patterns and religeous iconography. When we brought our work in a couple weeks before the opening, I was awe struck by how good his work was. I also realized that quanity wise, I was lacking. He had over 50 pieces to show. They were different sizes, shapes, themes, and subjects. The variety and quality was amazing. The contrast between his work and mine, made me feel... rather inadequate. But I told myself they were different mediums. I was still proud of my work. So I pushed that animal back down and kept it from consuming me. I did what I could to help, but the gallery is set up by the director. She does a great job, and takes the full two weeks to use the work we submit to fill the space so it works together. We can give input if we want, but I know as a trained and educated artist, she knows much more about this stuff than I do. I just glue things together and paint them. So I left it up to her.
I stopped in on Friday before the exhibition, and the impact of how limited my work was began to hit me more. My work seemed to almost disappear in the vast sea of the other artists bold and impressive work. I should have done more. I should have provided more of my work. I admit that I was limited by the number of display pedestals they had, but I could have pushed more to build my own pedestals to display my work, or some way to provide more room for my pieces. I did have a year. That's completely on me. Opening night came and at first, there were only a few people. Most of the members come to the openings, but many didn't for this one. It's the holiday season, so many people have other obligations. The other artist promoted his opening quite a bit on social media, with the help of his significant other. About a half-hour into it, people for his event began to file in. The room was full. Everyone was admiring his work and taking in the impact that it gave. I had provided small narratives of my work to describe how I got the idea, what I'd used to create it, or other information to help describe the piece. Since the work will be there until January, I wanted people to be able to read about the work without me present. For the opening, I'd prepared myself to talk to people who looked interested in my work, and answer any questions they might have. That proved to not be something I needed to do. Very few people seemed to be interested in my work. Many people used the pedestals to hold their drinks or food while they talked with others. I saw some children interested in a couple of my pieces, like the model train layout I had recently completed, but this only reinforced the idea in my head that my work is amateur, immature, and not a viable art medium. I attempted to talk to the other artist, but he was often busy talking to all the people who had shown up to see his work. The director did make a speech introducing us and describing our work. Obviously, he had much more to describe, and he was also personally known by many of the other artists in the gallery, since he was a tattoo artists as well, and had done personal work for them. Two people I knew showed up for my exhibit. I fully admit I didn't set up an event on social media like the other person had, but I'm also not great at self promotion. Three people asked me about my work, one of which was a child who kept asking me where I bought the models, and didn't seem to understand they didn't come painted or with the diorama already built. The next day, some people asked how my "thing" went. I told them it went fine. I haven't described it past that, because I simply cannot. I felt dismissed and unimportant on Saturday. I felt like the whole thing was a joke; a pity exhibit awarded to me because they needed floor space to even out the room. I left about 15 minutes before closing, because I was getting anxious about drinks and food being near my work, and seeing some of the pedestals wobbling after being bumped into, didn't want to witness seeing my work fall onto the floor. I don't think I would have reacted well. TL;DR - I put some of my scale models in an art gallery and it was generally ignored and dismissed. Thank you for letting me vent. Edit: I appreciate everyone's feedback and comments. I'm trying to read through all of them and reply or give answers if I can. But thank you for the support and kind words and above all else, your honesty.
item.dc:creator: PleaseNotTheFace

item.title: MONDAY Phone Clearing Meme Dump (and some GAMING memes)
item.description:
1
2. Why is everyone saying this is a great indie game? It was litteraly made by a team of 200 people and has Troy Baker in it!
3. Arise now, arise, Riders of Théoden! Dire deeds awake: dark is it eastward. Let horse be bridled, horn be sounded! Forth Eorlingas!
4. Hadouken
5. Your SPICY MEME OF THE DAY
6. Dude must be a blast at parties!
7. So which one are you taking?
8. Fun fact - this was considered impossible by the film makers (of the Fifth Element) until this lady did it.
9. GOT EM
10. ME: Student Loan Money WELL SPENT
11. Damn it, Never look into the barrel!
12. Weekends during winter
13. Pro tip: a new SSD Drive may save your laptop and make it serviceable for another 2-3 years
14. Alright, I just have everything set up perfectly, now time to restart Window-
15. Tell me about it! Anyone got an advice how to avoid scope creep? My first current project was supposed to be done in 4-5 months, but with me adding new content it almost 19 now. Still I hope players would appreciate what I'm building. For reference my first and second game took 4 and 8 months respectively. Also in the meantime I managed to make a prototype for a future game and release a free game on Itch! So anyway if you would like to support my work a simple wishlist will make all the difference: https://store.steampowered.com/app/2336120/Dont_Press_The_Button_To_Delete_The_Multiverse_The_Game/
item.dc:creator: TheseusGames
